API Design February 9, 2026

Experimenting with local LLM's

A few weeks ago I set up a local LLM environment using Ollama and Qwen2.5. In this post I share some of the early experiments and workflows I've found useful for testing and validating local LLM responses.

Local LLM's are a great way to experiment with model responses and test out different prompts without the latency and cost of hitting a remote API. Knowing there are no "out of pocket" costs or token limits puts my mind at ease and encourages more exploratory testing. By running the model locally, you can iterate much faster and have more control over the environment.

I've found this to be especially useful for testing edge cases and unexpected inputs. When working with a remote API, it can be cumbersome to set up test scenarios that trigger specific model behaviors. With a local LLM, I can easily craft prompts that explore those edge cases and see how the model responds in real time.

I’ve found this especially useful for seeded user flows and request combinations that are cumbersome to reproduce in shared lower environments. A focused mock layer can make both exploratory testing and automated coverage more intentional.

When the real service catches up, the transition is smoother because the expected behavior has already been exercised in code, UI states, and test scenarios instead of living only in documentation.
